1: Jethro, the priest of Midian (kohen Midyan), Moses's father-in-law, heard of all that God had done for Moses and for Israel his people, how YHWH had brought Israel out of Egypt: Jethro (Yitro = his excellence?; also called Reuel in Exodus 2:18 and Numbers 10:29; possibly the same person under different names, or the two names represent different traditions) is identified as kohen Midyan (priest of Midian) — a non-Israelite religious authority. The Midianites were a nomadic people of the northern Arabian/Sinai region, descendants of Abraham through Keturah (Genesis 25:2). The Midianite connection to Israel through Moses (who spent 40 years with Jethro's family) created a unique cultural-religious relationship that appears at several critical moments (the burning bush; the judicial reorganization here; the later Midianite apostasy of Num 25 that makes the Midianite relationship complex).
2: Now Jethro, Moses's father-in-law, had taken Zipporah, Moses's wife, after he had sent her home: The notation that Moses had 'sent away' (shillach = to send/divorce, the same technical term for divorce in Deuteronomy 24:1) Zipporah earlier is a gap in the narrative — there is no previous record of this sending-away. The most likely interpretation is that Moses sent Zipporah and the boys back to Midian at some point during the Egypt confrontations (possibly after the lodging-place circumcision incident of Exodus 4:24-26). This verse fills the gap retroactively, explaining why Jethro is now bringing Zipporah to Moses.
3: Along with her two sons. The name of the one was Gershom (for he said, 'I have been a sojourner in a foreign land'), Gershom (ger + sham = sojourner there) is the son whose name encodes Moses's identity as a perpetual exile — he was a sojourner in Midian, just as Israel was a sojourner in Egypt. The naming of Gershom (Exodus 2:22) is the first instance in the Exodus narrative of the ger (sojourner/alien) motif that will structure the ethical treatment of aliens in the Covenant Code (Exodus 22:21, Exodus 23:9).
4: And the name of the other, Eliezer (for he said, 'The God of my father was my help, and delivered me from the sword of Pharaoh'): Eliezer (El + ezer = my God is help) encodes the Exodus theology in a personal name — 'the God of my father' (Elohei avi) = the patriarchal God; 'delivered me from the sword of Pharaoh' = the specific rescue that preceded the Exodus (when Moses fled Pharaoh's sword after killing the Egyptian, Exodus 2:15). Moses named his son after a personal deliverance that preceded the national deliverance; the personal and national Exodus stories are nested within each other.
5: Jethro, Moses's father-in-law, came with his sons and his wife to Moses in the wilderness where he was encamped at the mountain of God: The mountain of God (har ha-Elohim) = Horeb/Sinai, the site of the burning bush (Exodus 3:1) and the destination of the Exodus journey ('you will serve God on this mountain,' Exodus 3:12). The reunion of Moses with his family at the mountain of God is the fulfillment of the burning-bush promise — Moses is back at the mountain, but now at the head of a redeemed nation. The circle of the burning-bush narrative closes at Sinai.
6: And when he sent word to Moses, 'I, your father-in-law Jethro, am coming to you with your wife and her two sons with her,' The formal announcement of Jethro's arrival follows ANE diplomatic protocol for important visits — a prior notification allowing the host to prepare a reception. The formality of the announcement contrasts with the intimacy of the family reunion in Exodus 18:7; the diplomatic frame acknowledges that Jethro comes as a representative figure (priest of Midian) as well as a family member.
7: Moses went out to meet his father-in-law and bowed down (va'yishtachu) and kissed him. And they asked each other of their welfare and went into the tent: Moses bowing (shachah = the full prostration-bow, the worship posture used for deity; also used for showing honor to humans) before his father-in-law reflects the honor culture of the ancient Near East in which a son-in-law showed deference to his wife's father. The mutual inquiry about welfare (va'yish'alu ish-l'ree'eihu l'shalom = they asked each other about peace/welfare) uses shalom as the greeting-word, connecting personal relational peace with the covenant peace that Israel has just secured through the Exodus.
8: Then Moses told his father-in-law all that YHWH had done to Pharaoh and to the Egyptians for Israel's sake, all the hardship that had come upon them in the way, and how YHWH had delivered them: Moses's recounting of the Exodus story to Jethro is the first 'testimony' narration in the Exodus narrative — the eyewitness telling of YHWH's saving acts to an outsider. The structure (what YHWH did to Egypt + the hardship on the journey + YHWH's deliverance) is the proto-form of the Exodus creed that will be recited in Deuteronomy 6:20-25 and Exodus 26:5-9. The narrative function of telling the Exodus story to Jethro produces the first Gentile faith-response (Exodus 18:11).
9: And Jethro rejoiced (vayyichdah) for all the good that YHWH had done to Israel, in that he had delivered them out of the hand of the Egyptians: Chadah = to rejoice/be glad (a relatively rare verb; the cognate in Aramaic is cheda = joy). Jethro's joy for Israel's deliverance is the emotional prerequisite for his coming confession in Exodus 18:11. The outsider who rejoices for YHWH's acts on behalf of his people is already moving toward faith — Jethro's joy is the precursor to his theological affirmation.
10: Jethro said, 'Blessed be YHWH (baruch YHWH), who has delivered you out of the hand of the Egyptians and out of the hand of Pharaoh and has delivered the people from under the hand of the Egyptians.' The baruch YHWH formula (blessed be YHWH) is the standard Hebrew doxology, used by Melchizedek (Genesis 14:20), Naomi (Ruth 2:20), and throughout the Psalms. Jethro the Midianite priest uses the covenant name YHWH (not El, not Elohim) — suggesting either that the Midianites already had knowledge of the YHWH name (the 'Kenite hypothesis' that Moses encountered YHWH-worship among the Midianites) or that Moses has been reporting YHWH's name to Jethro in the recounting of Exodus 18:8.
11: 'Now I know (ata yadati) that YHWH is greater than all gods (gadol YHWH mikol ha-elohim), for in this affair they dealt arrogantly with the people.' Jethro's confession is the first Gentile doxology in the Exodus narrative and one of the earliest monotheistic (or henotheistic) declarations from a non-Israelite perspective. Ata yadati = 'now I know' — experiential knowledge (da'at) produced by hearing the Exodus testimony. Gadol YHWH mikol ha-elohim = 'YHWH is greater than all the gods' — the incomparability formula (cf. mi-kamokha ba'elim YHWH, Exodus 15:11) now made by an outsider. The 'Kenite hypothesis' (that Moses encountered YHWH-worship among the Midianites and that Jethro's priesthood was already YHWH-focused) is supported by this verse's confident use of the covenant name.
12: And Jethro, Moses's father-in-law, brought a burnt offering (olah) and sacrifices (zevachim) to God. And Aaron came with all the elders of Israel to eat bread with Moses's father-in-law before God: Jethro officiates at a sacrifice before God — and Aaron (Israel's future high priest) and all the elders eat the covenant meal with Jethro. The shared meal 'before God' (lifnei ha-Elohim) is covenant-communion language; eating together before YHWH ratifies a relationship. The inclusion of Israel's entire leadership in Jethro's sacrificial meal is a remarkable act of Gentile-Israelite covenant fellowship that has no parallel elsewhere in the Pentateuch. Whether Jethro's priesthood was formally recognized as valid is debated, but the text clearly treats his sacrifice as acceptable to YHWH.
13: The next day Moses sat to judge the people, and the people stood around Moses from morning till evening: Moses's role as sole judge for the entire Israelite community — 600,000 men plus dependents — is clearly unsustainable at any level of case complexity. The morning-to-evening session describes a genuinely exhausting system: every dispute in a community of perhaps 2-3 million people eventually reached Moses as the sole arbiter. Ancient Near Eastern legal systems typically involved multiple tiers of authority: local elders → city judges → royal courts. Moses's single-tier system is anomalous by ANE standards.
14: When Moses's father-in-law saw all that he was doing for the people, he said, 'What is this that you are doing for the people? Why do you sit alone, and all the people stand around you from morning till evening?' Jethro observes the system and immediately diagnoses its unsustainability. His outsider perspective gives him a clarity that Moses, embedded in the system, lacks. The 'why do you sit alone?' (madua' ata yoshev l'vadecha) is the same 'alone' (levad) that appears in the creation narrative — 'it is not good for man to be alone' (Genesis 2:18). The institutional aloneness of Moses is as unsustainable as the human aloneness that prompted creation of woman.
15: And Moses said to his father-in-law, 'Because the people come to me to inquire of God (lidrosh Elohim); when they have a dispute, they come to me and I decide between one person and another, and I make them know the statutes of God and his laws.' Darash Elohim = to seek/inquire of God — the technical term for consulting an oracle or divine authority. Moses frames his role as more than judicial: he is the mediator of divine instruction (chuqqei ha-Elohim ve-torotav = the statutes of God and his laws). The judicial role is inseparable from the teaching role; deciding cases is simultaneously teaching the covenant law. This dual function (judge + teacher) is the model for the Levitical priesthood (Deuteronomy 33:10) and the prophetic office.
16: 'When they have a dispute, they come to me and I decide between one person and another, and I make them know the statutes of God and his laws.' The repeated description of Moses's dual function (deciding disputes + teaching statutes) emphasizes that judicial administration and covenant instruction are unified in his role. The separation of the judicial and teaching functions that Jethro will propose (delegates handle cases; Moses teaches law) preserves the unity of the original role by distributing its applications.
17: Moses's father-in-law said to him, 'What you are doing is not good.' The blunt assessment lo-tov (not good) uses the same phrase as Genesis 2:18 ('it is not good for man to be alone'). Jethro's 'not good' is institutional wisdom expressed in creation-theology language: the current structure is as contrary to human flourishing as the aloneness it took woman to resolve. The structural unsustainability is framed in terms of human good (tov), not merely organizational efficiency.
18: 'You and the people with you will certainly wear out (naval tibbol), for the thing is too heavy (kaved) for you. You are not able to do it alone.' Naval = to droop/be worn out/fade (the same root as naval = foolish — the name Nabal in 1 Sam 25 who 'wears out' those who deal with him); the doubled infinitive absolute (naval tibbol) is emphatic: you will surely be exhausted. Kaved = heavy (the same root as kavod/glory and the hardening of Pharaoh's heart) — the administrative burden is now 'heavy' on Moses in the same way Pharaoh's heart was 'heavy.' The organizational crisis mirrors the theological crisis of the plagues.
19: 'Now obey my voice; I will give you advice, and God be with you! You shall represent the people before God and bring their cases to God.' Jethro's counsel is explicitly submitted to divine oversight ('God be with you') — he is not imposing a secular administrative reorganization but proposing a structure within YHWH's framework. The proposal preserves Moses's irreplaceable role (representing the people before God, v'heyita ata la'am mul ha-Elohim = you being for the people opposite/before God) while distributing the derivative functions to others.
20: 'And you shall warn them about the statutes and the laws, and make them know the way in which they must walk and what they must do.' The four verbs of Moses's teaching function: warn (v'hizharta = from zahar, to shine/warn), make them know (v'hoda'ta = from yadah, to know), the way (ha-derech), and what to do (ha-ma'aseh). The teaching role is inseparable from the judicial role — Moses must continue to be the primary instructor of Torah even after he delegates the case-load. The Hebrew 'way' (derech) and 'deeds' (ma'aseh) encompass both ethical direction and practical application.
21: 'Moreover, look for able men from all the people, men who fear God, who are trustworthy and hate a bribe, and place such men over the people as chiefs of thousands, hundreds, fifties, and tens.' The four qualifications for judges: able (chayil = capable/valiant), God-fearing (yire'ei Elohim), trustworthy (anshei emet = men of truth/faithfulness), hating dishonest gain (son'ei vatza = hating unjust profit). The hierarchical structure (thousands, hundreds, fifties, tens) becomes the organizational framework for the Israelite military (Numbers 31:14), the early monarchy (2 Samuel 18:1), and the New Testament church governance (the apostolic appointment of deacons echoes this structure, Acts 6:1-7). The Qumran community (Dead Sea Scrolls) also organized itself along this hierarchical model.
22: 'And let them judge the people at all times. Every great matter they shall bring to you, but any small matter they shall decide themselves. So it will be easier for you, and they will bear the burden with you.' The delegation principle: subsidiarity — decisions are made at the lowest competent level. Only 'great matters' (ha-davar ha-gadol) reach Moses; small matters (kol-ha-davar ha-qaton) are handled locally. This principle of subsidiarity (delegation to the most local competent authority) is embedded in Israelite law and governance from Sinai forward. It also ensures that Moses's authoritative rulings set precedents for the most complex and consequential cases, making his decisions constitutional in character.
23: 'If you do this, God will direct you, you will be able to endure, and all this people also will go to their place in peace.' The two outcomes of the reorganization: Moses's endurance (tuchal amod) and the people's peaceful return to their places (kol-ha'am ha-zeh al-m'qomo yavo b'shalom). The shalom of the people's daily life depends on a sustainable judicial system. The connection between institutional health and communal peace (shalom) — individual welfare requires structured, accessible justice — is a foundational principle of Israelite social theology.
24: So Moses listened to the voice of his father-in-law and did all that he had said: Moses's willingness to receive counsel from a Gentile father-in-law and implement his organizational recommendations is a remarkable display of humility. The greatest leader in Israelite history accepts institutional wisdom from an outsider. Numbers 12:3 ('Moses was very humble, more than all people on the face of the earth') is illustrated here practically: the humble receive counsel even from unexpected sources.
25: Moses chose able men out of all Israel and made them heads over the people, chiefs of thousands, of hundreds, of fifties, and of tens: The implementation of Jethro's proposal using the exact vocabulary of his recommendation (chayil = able, alafim/me'ot/chamishim/asarot = thousands/hundreds/fifties/tens) demonstrates full compliance. The judicial reorganization of Israel creates the first formal governmental structure of the Israelite community — the tribal elders and clan heads already existed, but this creates a formal judicial hierarchy with defined jurisdictions.
26: And they judged the people at all times. Any hard case (ha-davar ha-kashe) they brought to Moses, but any small matter they decided themselves: The system in operation: the hard cases (kashe = difficult/hard — the same root as qashah in Pharaoh's stubbornness) escalate to Moses; the small cases are handled locally. The distribution of juridical difficulty mirrors the distribution of spiritual difficulty: the 'hard cases' that require direct access to YHWH's revealed instruction remain Moses's domain, while the 'small cases' that can be decided by applying known Torah principles are handled by the delegate judges.
27: Then Moses let his father-in-law depart, and he went away to his own country: Jethro returns to Midian after his transformative visit — he came as the priest of Midian, confessed YHWH's supremacy, reorganized Israel's judiciary, and departed. The brevity of his departure (one verse) after his extensive contribution (the entire chapter) is characteristically Hebraic: the narrative does not dwell on farewells. Jethro disappears from the Exodus narrative after Exodus 18; his son Hobab appears in Numbers 10:29 as a potential guide for Israel in the wilderness, but whether Hobab was persuaded to stay is itself uncertain (Numbers 10:30-32).
